A message goes to the Crown's Justice by fishing boat. Nothing written — a verbal message, delivered by a harbour boy who cannot read and does not know what the words mean.

The response comes within two hours. A boat from the Crown's Justice, a lieutenant you have seen before, a sealed note in Ashford's handwriting.

*Come aboard. White flag. Bring what you found.*

Direct and unambiguous. Ashford knows you went to the San Cristobal. He knows you found something. He wants to deal.

choices.text: Go aboard the Crown's Justice
Go aboard — bring the war chest
Go aboard empty-handed — negotiate with nothing

r_51.text:
The Crown's Justice's great cabin. Ashford behind his desk. Marines at the door. The lantern swings with the harbour swell.

You set the brass tube on the desk between you. Ashford looks at it. He knows what it is — he has been waiting ten years for someone to find it.

"The Red Correspondence," you say. "Your letters to Jamaica. The prize claim. The two hundred and fourteen men you put to the boats."

Ashford's face does not change. He is a man who has been expecting this moment for a decade.

"What do you want?" he asks.

You can see the calculation behind his eyes. If those letters reach London, he hangs. Not metaphorically. Actually hangs. A commodore executed for murder and theft — the Admiralty would make an example.

choices.text: Offer the deal — you keep the letters, he leaves Nassau
Demand his unconditional withdrawal
This was a mistake — you should have used the letters publicly

r_52.text:
"You leave," you say. "All four ships. Nassau is not blockaded, not raided, not interfered with. In return, the letters stay in Nassau. Insurance, not ammunition. As long as you leave us alone, they stay sealed."

Ashford considers. You can see him running the numbers — the risk of the letters against the cost of withdrawal against the damage to his career if he returns to London empty-handed.

"And the gold?" he asks. "The war chest?"

"Was never yours," you say. "It belongs to the Jamaica garrison. Or it did. Now it belongs to whoever holds it."

A long silence. The ship creaks around you.

"Agreed," Ashford says. "My ships will clear the harbour mouth by morning."

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_53.text:
"Unconditional," you say. "You withdraw. All four ships. No terms, no conditions, no return. If I see Crown sails off Nassau again, the letters go to London on the next packet ship."

Ashford's jaw works. This is harder for him — not the withdrawal, but the submission. He is a commodore. He gives orders. He does not take them from pirates.

"You are making an enemy," he says quietly.

"You were already an enemy," you say. "Now you are an enemy who is leaving."

Another silence. Longer this time. Then Ashford stands.

"My ships will be gone by sunset," he says. His voice is flat. "Do not contact me again."

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_54.text:
You reach for the brass tube. A mistake — Ashford's hand moves faster.

He does not draw a weapon. He does not need to. He speaks one word — "Marines" — and the door opens and two armed men step inside with muskets levelled.

"You came aboard my ship under a flag of truce," Ashford says. His voice has changed — the careful negotiator is gone. This is the officer. "You brought evidence of a crime that, if made public, ends my career and my life. And now you are reaching for it with an expression that suggests you intend to leave."

He leans forward. "You will not leave."

The marines move. You are disarmed, efficiently, without violence. Your cutlass and flintlock are taken. The brass tube stays on the desk.

Ashford sits down and begins writing. A report, you realise. Your capture. Your crimes. The legal language of piracy — taken in lawful action, subject to Crown justice.

choices.text: There is no escape from this

r_55.text:
You go aboard the Crown's Justice with nothing. No letters. No gold. No leverage.

Ashford receives you correctly — the formalities of a flag of truce observed. He leads you to the great cabin and sits behind his desk.

"Captain," he says. "You are aboard my ship. You have requested this meeting. What do you have to say?"

You have nothing to offer and nothing to threaten. The conversation is short. Ashford listens. He is polite. He is patient. He is immovable.

"Nassau will accept the Crown's terms or Nassau will be taken," he says. "I have four ships and six hundred marines. You have — what? A dozen merchant vessels with popguns? Fishermen with cutlasses?"

He stands. "I will give you until morning to reconsider. After that, I come ashore."

The marines escort you back to the rail.

choices.text: Return to Nassau — you need another plan
Draw your flintlock — one shot

r_56.text:
You return to Nassau empty-handed. The town is quiet. The captains are waiting for news.

You have no news. Ashford will come ashore by morning. Nassau has nothing to stop him.

Unless you can find another path.

choices.text: Use the Red Correspondence publicly
Launch a desperate assault with the Dragon's Breath
Accept that Nassau is lost
Sail the Adamant out — save your ship and crew

r_57.text:
You draw the flintlock.

One shot. You have always had one shot. The pistol has been in your belt since you first stepped aboard the Adamant, cleaned and loaded, the flint sharp, the powder dry. A reliable weapon. One shot, well-placed, ends most arguments before they start.

The marines see the movement. Ashford sees it. Time stretches.

You level the flintlock at Ashford's chest. He does not flinch. He looks at you with the expression of a man who has been shot at before and survived it.

"If you fire," he says, "my marines will kill you. Your ship will be taken. Nassau will burn. One pistol ball does not change the arithmetic."

He is right. You know he is right. One shot changes nothing — except the nature of the ending.

choices.text: Fire — and accept the consequences
Lower the pistol — he is right

r_58.text:
You fire.

The sound is enormous in the closed cabin. Smoke fills the space. The ball hits Ashford in the left shoulder — not the chest, not where you aimed. The ship moved. The shot went wide.

Ashford staggers back, gripping the desk. Blood darkens his uniform. His face goes white, but he does not fall.

"Marines," he says through his teeth.

They are already moving. The cabin door bursts open. Muskets level. You have an empty pistol and a cutlass against six armed men in an enclosed space.

There is no winning this. There was never going to be.

r_58a.text:
You cut down the first marine. The second hesitates — long enough for you to reach the stern gallery. The glass shatters under your boot and you are through it, falling, hitting the water twenty feet below.

The harbour swallows you. Above, musket fire cracks across the water. Balls hit the surface around you in white plumes. You dive deep, lungs burning, swimming blind.

When you surface, gasping, you are fifty yards from the Crown's Justice. The Adamant's launch is waiting — Pell, who saw the muzzle flash from the cabin and brought the boat around.

"Captain," he says, hauling you aboard. "That was either very brave or very stupid."

"Both," you manage.

Ashford is alive. Wounded. Furious. His marines are lining the rail, reloading. But you are out of range, and the launch is pulling for Nassau.

One shot. It did not end the argument. But it changed the terms.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_59.text:
You lower the pistol.

The marines do not relax. Ashford does not blink. He waits until the flintlock is at your side, then nods once.

"A sensible decision," he says. "You may return to your ship. My offer stands — you have until morning."

The marines escort you to the rail. You climb down to the launch with your flintlock still in your belt, unfired. One shot, still loaded. You did not use it.

Was it wisdom or cowardice? Standing in the boat, watching the Crown's Justice recede, you are not certain.

choices.text: Return to Nassau

r_60.text:
You have nothing. No letters. No gold. No allies.

Nassau is dark and quiet. The waterfront is mostly empty — ships gone, crews scattered, the harbour a shadow of what it was. Ashford's blockade has been strangling the town for weeks and you can see it in the closed shops, the empty taverns, the way people move with their heads down.

You walk the waterfront alone. Your crew is aboard the Adamant, waiting. The town is waiting. Everyone is waiting for something to happen or for nothing to happen, which amounts to the same thing.

Without leverage, without allies, without resources — what can you do?

choices.text: Try to rally the captains anyway
Attempt to negotiate with Ashford directly
Take the Adamant and run — save your ship

r_62.text:
You send word to Ashford. Not terms. Not negotiation. Capitulation.

The message is simple: Nassau will accept the Crown's authority. The harbour is open. In return, you want safe passage for the Adamant and her crew.

Ashford's response comes within the hour. Agreed. The Adamant may leave. Nassau is his.

The captains do not know until they see the Crown's boats coming ashore. By then it is too late. Marines on the waterfront. The Union flag over the fort. Nassau's brief experiment in freedom, over.

You watch from the Adamant's quarterdeck as Nassau becomes a Crown port again. The war chest is in the hold — your passage out, your future, someone else's gold.

The harbour mouth is open. The Adamant makes sail on the evening tide.

choices.text: Sail away — Nassau is behind you

r_63.text:
You take the Adamant out through the back channel at night. The same route in, in reverse — coral scraping the keel, phosphorescence marking the reef edges, Pell calling depth in a whisper.

Nassau falls behind you. The blockade lights recede. The open sea opens.

You did not save Nassau. You did not try hard enough, or you tried and failed, or you decided that saving yourself was more important. The crew does not judge you — they are alive, the ship is whole, the horizon is open.

But something is left behind. Not guilt exactly. Something more like a question that will never be answered — what would have happened if you had stayed?

The wind fills the Adamant's sails. You set a course south. Nassau is someone else's problem now.

choices.text: The sea takes you

r_64.text:
You are moving through the waterfront district in the early morning hours, heading for the fort, when you feel it — a wrongness. Too quiet. The tavern on the corner, which should be serving the night watch, is shuttered and dark. The lantern at the harbour master's post has been extinguished.

You stop. Listen.

Footsteps behind you. More than one pair. Moving carefully on the wet stones.

You turn. Three men in the alley mouth, backlit by the harbour. One of them carries a boarding axe. Another has a club.

The third steps forward. In the dim light, you recognise him — a harbour man, one of the Council's factors. Someone you spoke to. Someone who knew your plans.

"Nothing personal, Captain," he says. "But there is a man on the blockade ships paying good gold for information about you. And better gold for you, personally."

He nods to the other two. They move forward.

choices.text: Fight your way through
Run for the waterfront

r_65.text: You draw your cutlass. The alley is narrow — that helps. They cannot come at you together.

r_66.text:
You run. The wet stones are treacherous — your boots slip once, twice. Behind you, the men give chase.

The waterfront is close. If you can reach the Adamant's dock, your crew will see you. The ship's bell will bring them running.

test_luck.lucky_text: You make it. The dock appears out of the dark, the Adamant's lanterns at the end, and you are running full tilt when Pell sees you from the rail. He shouts. The crew comes alive. The men chasing you see the movement aboard the ship and fall back into the dark. You are safe — but someone in Nassau tried to sell you to Ashford.
test_luck.unlucky_text: Your boot catches a mooring ring in the dark. You go down hard, hands out, chin hitting stone. The world flashes white. When it clears, they are on you. Hands pulling you up. A cloth over your face, smelling of something sweet and chemical. The harbour tilts sideways. The dark closes in.

r_67.text:
You survive the ambush. But the message is clear — someone in Nassau is working for Ashford. The Council's factor, the man who tried to have you taken. Ashford is not just waiting. He is buying.

You return to the Adamant, shaken. Pell has the crew at stations, watching the waterfront.

"We are not safe on shore, Captain," he says. It is not a complaint. It is a fact.

You clean the scrape on your chin and think about what this means. Nassau is not united. Nassau has fractures, and Ashford is finding them.

The rally is more important than ever. Or the whole thing falls apart.

choices.text: Prepare for the rally — there is no more time

r_70.text:
Nassau harbour. The blockade is gone. The harbour mouth stands open — blue water, white caps, the horizon unbroken for the first time in weeks.

The captains are on the waterfront. Some of them are cheering. Some are quiet, watching the empty sea where Ashford's ships were, not quite believing it.

You stand on the fort wall. Below you, the harbour. The Adamant at anchor, her crew on deck, looking up at you. The other ships — eleven of them, or eight, depending on how the rally went — at their moorings, flags flying.

Nassau is free. The question is what kind of freedom.

choices.text: Find Morrow — she stood with you by choice

r_71.text:
Morrow is on the wall. She came up while you were watching the harbour, and she is standing a few paces away, looking out at the sea.

She does not speak immediately. The wind moves her coat. Her hair. The empty harbour mouth where four ships used to be.

"I did not expect to be here," she says finally. Not looking at you. Looking out. "I expected to be running. Or dead. Or somewhere on the other side of the Atlantic, pretending none of this happened."

She turns to you. Her expression is not quite respect and not quite gratitude. It is something more complicated — the face of a person who was given a choice they did not expect and made the right one and is still surprised by it.

"You could have used what you knew about me," she says. "You chose not to. I am standing here because of that choice. I want you to know I understand what it cost you — the leverage you gave up. The risk."

She looks back at the harbour. "Nassau remembers who stood on the wall, Captain. I will make sure of it."

She does not say thank you. She does not need to.

choices.text: Stand together on the wall — Nassau is free
Return to the Adamant

r_72.text:
Morrow is at the Broken Wheel. She is drinking alone. She does not look up when you enter.

"It is over," you say.

She nods. Drinks. Sets the cup down.

"I stood where you told me to stand," she says. "I did what you needed me to do. Nassau is free. Are we finished?"

There is no warmth in it. She did her part because you forced her hand. She kept her word. But she will not pretend it was willing.

"We are finished," you say.

She nods again. "Then I have a ship to see to." She stands, drops a coin on the bar, and walks past you without looking back.

Morrow kept her word. That is all it was — a kept word, nothing more.

choices.text: Return to the fort
Return to the Adamant

r_73.text:
You find Morrow at the harbour. She is watching her own ship — the Celestine — being resupplied. When she sees you, she nods.

"Well done, Captain," she says. Professional. Sincere. Not warm, but genuine. "You did what you said you would do. Nassau is still standing."

She extends her hand. You shake it. The grip is firm and brief.

"If you need me again," she says, "you know where to find me. Nassau captains look out for each other."

She turns back to her ship. The conversation is over. It was enough.

choices.text: Address the captains from the fort wall
Return to the Adamant

r_74.text:
You address the captains from the fort wall. The same platform where the rally was held — the same stone, the same view of the harbour. But the harbour mouth is open now, and the captains are not frightened. They are free.

"Captains," you say. "The blockade is broken. Ashford is gone. Nassau stands."

The cheer that goes up is not organized. It is ragged, spontaneous, loud. Men who have been holding their breath for weeks finally letting it out.

"What happens next is up to you," you continue. "Nassau was always its own republic. It does not need a saviour. It needs captains who stay, who trade, who build. Who remember what this harbour is for."

Delacroix raises a bottle. Oakes claps you on the shoulder. Villiers nods from the back of the crowd — her approval harder to earn and more valuable for it.

Nassau is free. And it knows it.

choices.text: Find Morrow on the wall — she earned this

Accept what you have built here

r_75.text:
The Adamant. Your ship. Your crew.

Pell is on the quarterdeck, looking at you. "Orders, Captain?"

The harbour mouth is open. The wind is fair. The Adamant is provisioned and ready to sail. Nassau is behind you — free, chaotic, imperfect, surviving.

You can stay. You can go. The sea does not care which. The sea only cares that you choose.

choices.text: Stay in Nassau — this is home now
Set sail — the horizon is calling
Set sail — there is nothing left for you here
Set sail — leave it all behind

r_76.text:
You stand on the fort wall. Nassau is spread below you — the harbour, the ships, the waterfront coming alive again. Smoke from cook fires. The sound of hammers on hulls as ships are repaired. The ordinary sounds of a town that has survived.

Morrow is beside you. She came up to the wall without being asked, and she stands there — not speaking, not needing to speak. The wind off the harbour smells of salt and tar and something underneath that is not sweet, exactly, but is Nassau.

The Red Correspondence has been read. Ashford is gone. The captains are committed. The Harbour Council's resources are flowing. Everything that could be done right was done right.

This is the ending. Not a perfect one — there is no such thing. But a true one.

choices.text: Nassau remembers who stood on the wall
Nassau remembers

r_77.text:
Nassau is free. Ashford has been exposed. The captains rallied.

But something is missing. Morrow is not beside you — she is in the crowd somewhere, or on her ship, or gone. The victory is real. The cost is real. And the space where a particular person should be standing is empty.

You did what you came to do. Nassau is saved. The question is whether you saved everything that mattered.

choices.text: Accept the victory for what it is

end_saviour_true.text:
Nassau's captains stand on the fort walls and watch the last of Ashford's ships slip below the horizon. The harbour mouth is open — blue water, white light, the sea stretching out in every direction without obstruction. For the first time in weeks, boats are moving freely through the channel. A fishing sloop puts out on the morning tide. A merchant brig begins loading cargo. The ordinary machinery of a free port, turning again.

Morrow is beside you on the wall. She stands with her hands on the stone parapet, looking out at the water. She did not have to be here — you gave her the choice, and she made it, and you have come to understand that this is the difference between a republic that lasts and one that does not. Not the walls. Not the guns. Not even the gold. The choice. The willingness to stand when standing costs something.

Below, in the harbour square, the Red Correspondence has been posted for all to read. Ashford's name is in every sentence — the prize claim, the two hundred and fourteen men, the decade of silence. His ships are gone. His career is over. His crimes are public record now, written in salt-stained ink on the customs house door where every captain in the Caribbean will read them.

The Harbour Council's resources are flowing — ships refitted, crews paid, powder and shot stockpiled in the fort's magazine. The harbour bond you secured has turned words into material reality. Nassau is not just free. Nassau is provisioned, armed, and backed by its own merchants.

Morrow steps back from the wall. She looks at you with something that is not quite respect and not quite gratitude — something more complicated than either, something that has no word in any language you speak. She holds your gaze for a long moment.

"Nassau will remember who stood on the wall," she says. And then, quieter: "So will I."

She walks away. You watch her go — down the fort stairs, across the square, toward the harbour. Her ship is there. Her crew. Her life, which you could have destroyed and chose not to.

You turn back to the sea. The horizon is empty and open and belongs to no one. Nassau is at your back, loud and alive and free. The Adamant is at anchor below, her crew on deck, her sails furled, waiting for your next order.

But for now, you stand on the wall. The sun is warm. The wind smells of salt. And Nassau — imperfect, ungovernable, stubbornly free — is still standing.

Because you stood first.

end_saviour.text:
Nassau is saved.

You stand on the fort wall and watch the harbour come alive again. Ships moving, crews working, the waterfront filling with the noise and commerce of a free port. The blockade is broken. Ashford's name is posted in the harbour square — the Red Correspondence, copied and distributed, nailed to every public surface in the town. His crimes are known. His ships are gone.

The captains rallied. They stood together when it mattered, and that is not nothing — it may be the thing that saves Nassau again, the next time. Because there will be a next time. There is always a next time.

But the wall beside you is empty. Morrow is not here. She is somewhere in the crowd, or aboard her ship, or gone — you do not know which. She helped. She stood where she needed to stand. But the distance between you was never closed, and it will not be closed now.

The victory is real. You earned it. The Red Correspondence broke Ashford's blockade more thoroughly than any broadside could have. The captains know the truth. The harbour knows the truth. And the truth is Nassau's best defense — because the next Crown officer who considers blockading this port will know that Nassau keeps records and Nassau has long memory.

You did what you came to do. Nassau is free. The republic holds.

It is enough. It is nearly enough.

You turn from the wall and walk down to the harbour. The Adamant is waiting. Your crew is waiting. The sea is open, and whatever comes next, you face it as the captain who saved Nassau.

They will remember that. Even if they forget everything else.

end_free_captain.text:
The harbour mouth is open. The wind is fair. The Adamant sits at anchor with her sails ready to drop, and your crew stands at their stations with the particular alertness of men who know they have won something.

Nassau rallied. The captains stood together. The blockade broke — not cleanly, not without cost, but it broke. And now the question is simple: what does a free captain do with freedom?

The war chest is gone — spent on Nassau's defence, or traded for Ashford's withdrawal, or divided among the crews who fought. The gold did its work. It bought time, or loyalty, or passage. You do not regret it. Gold is heavy and freedom is not, and you have always known which matters more.

Pell stands at the helm. He does not ask for orders. He waits. The crew waits. The Adamant waits — she has been waiting for this since you first brought her into Nassau harbour, weeks ago, in another life.

You look at Nassau one last time. The fort on the hill. The harbour below. The waterfront where you walked and talked and fought and made choices that you will carry for the rest of your life. It is a good harbour. It is not your harbour.

Yours is wherever the Adamant takes you.

"Make sail," you say.

The crew moves. Canvas drops and fills. The Adamant turns her bow toward the open sea, and the harbour falls behind you — the fort growing smaller, the ships at anchor shrinking, Nassau becoming a shape on the horizon and then a memory.

You are a free captain on an open sea. Whatever that means — whatever it costs — it is enough.

end_deal.text:
The blockade is gone. Ashford has withdrawn. Nassau survives.

But the survival has a price, and the price is silence.

You stand on the Adamant's quarterdeck and look at the harbour. Ships moving, crews working, the ordinary sounds of a port that has been given a reprieve it does not fully understand. The captains think the rally did it. They think their unity, their courage, their willingness to stand together drove Ashford away.

You know better. The deal was made in private — the Red Correspondence for Ashford's withdrawal. A secret exchanged for a secret. The letters stay in Nassau, sealed, insurance against a return that will never come because Ashford is finished either way. His career is over. His reputation is ash. But it happened quietly, and that is the point.

No one is satisfied. The captains have a victory that is not quite real. You have a peace that is not quite honest. Ashford has his life, which is not quite worth living. And Nassau has its freedom, which is built on a silence that can never be broken.

You told yourself, once, that the truth matters. That the Red Correspondence was important because it was true — because two hundred and fourteen men died and someone should answer for it. No one answered. The truth was used as a lever, not as justice.

Pell brings you coffee. The harbour is quiet. The morning is grey.

"What now, Captain?" he asks.

What now. The question of a man who has survived something and is not sure it was worth surviving. You drink your coffee. You watch the harbour. You keep the secret.

Nassau endures. That will have to be enough.

end_betrayal.text:
The Adamant clears Nassau on a running tide. The back channel swallows you — coral and dark water and the phosphorescence that marks the reef edges like a path through the underworld. Behind you, Nassau's lights dim and disappear.

You stand at the stern rail and watch them go. The fort. The harbour. The waterfront where captains are waking up to discover that the world has changed while they slept — that someone made a deal, or failed to make a stand, or simply left.

The war chest is in the hold. Crown gold, heavy and specific, someone else's fortune funding your escape. You tell yourself that is what the sea asks of a captain — hard choices, clean breaks, the willingness to cut loose when the tide turns against you. It is what every pirate learns or dies learning: nothing is yours. Not the harbour. Not the town. Not the men who trusted you. Everything is borrowed, and the sea collects its debts.

By the time land disappears, you almost believe it.

Pell does not speak. The crew does not speak. They are alive. The ship is whole. The horizon is open and empty and free, and freedom is what you wanted — what you have always wanted — and this is what it looks like.

Nassau is someone else's problem now. The captains will find new leaders or they will scatter or they will surrender. The republic will hold or it will not. It was always fragile — a collection of men who agreed on nothing except that they would rather be free than safe, and when the cost of freedom came due, you decided it was too high.

The wind fills the sails. The Adamant heels to starboard and the wake stretches out behind you, white on dark water, dissolving. You set a course south. Or east. Or wherever the wind takes you.

Nassau was a place. The sea is a direction.

You choose the direction.

end_pyrrhic.text:
The Adamant is gone.

She burns at the harbour mouth — a column of smoke and fire that rises straight up in the windless morning air. The flames have reached the mainmast now, and it stands like a torch, outlined against the dawn sky. You can hear the fire from the shore — the crack of timbers, the hiss of water turning to steam, the deep structural groaning of a ship that is dying.

You sit on the fort wall with nothing but what you are wearing — a salt-crusted coat, empty pockets, boots full of harbour water. Your cutlass is somewhere on the sea floor. Your pistol is gone. Your ship is burning.

But Ashford's ships are withdrawing.

The Crown's Justice limps toward the horizon, listing badly, her pumps fighting a losing battle against the hole the Dragon's Breath tore in her side. The Resolve has struck her colours. The Perseverance and Fervent are already gone — fled when the explosions started, their captains calculating that a crippled commodore is not worth dying for.

Nassau is free. The blockade is broken. The harbour mouth will be clear by noon, once the Adamant finishes burning.

You watch your ship die. She was a good ship — fast, loyal in the way that ships are loyal, responsive to the helm, forgiving of mistakes. Three years you sailed her. Three years of storms and calms and harbours and the particular partnership between a captain and a vessel that only sailors understand.

She bought Nassau's freedom. It was not a fair trade. Nothing out here is fair.

Delacroix sits down beside you. He has a bottle. He offers it. You drink.

"We won," he says.

"We won," you agree.

The Adamant burns. The harbour opens. Nassau is free, and you have nothing — no ship, no gold, no weapon, nothing but a coat full of salt water and the knowledge that you did what was necessary.

You decide that is enough.

It nearly is.

death_betrayed.text:
You wake in darkness. Your head aches — a deep, throbbing pain at the base of your skull. Your hands are tied behind your back. You are lying on wet stone, and the air smells of mould and low tide.

A cellar. Somewhere near the waterfront, from the sound of water against stone.

The door opens. Light falls across the floor. A man stands in the doorway — the Council's factor, the one who set the ambush. He looks at you without expression.

"I told you it was not personal," he says. "Ashford pays well. Better than Nassau, certainly. Better than whatever you thought you were building here."

Behind him, shadows. More men. The sound of a boat being made ready.

You are taken out before dawn. A fishing boat, running for the blockade. The Crown's Justice receives you at first light — marines on the rail, efficient, professional. They have done this before.

Nassau goes on. The captains will find out in the morning. They will be angry, then practical. Someone will take your place. Someone always takes your place.

Nassau goes on. It does not go on for you.

The charges are read aboard the Crown's Justice. Piracy. Theft of Crown property. Assault on His Majesty's forces. The legal language is meticulous, every word chosen by a man who understands that paperwork outlasts everything.

Your name appears in Crown records as a pirate taken in lawful action. The date is noted. The disposition is noted. The file is closed.

Nassau's harbour is visible through the stern gallery windows. So close. So completely out of reach.

death_ashford.text:
Ashford's marines are thorough.

You are disarmed, searched, and escorted to the brig in under three minutes. The cell is small, clean, and has a lock that a professional locksmith would admire. The marines do not speak. They do not need to — every movement is practiced, efficient, the product of years of training and the particular competence of men who serve under a commodore who tolerates nothing less.

Ashford visits you once. He stands outside the cell in his dress uniform, hands behind his back, and looks at you the way an entomologist looks at a pinned specimen.

"You came aboard my ship," he says. "Under a flag of truce, which I honoured. And you attempted to use stolen Crown documents as leverage against a serving officer of His Majesty's Navy."

He pauses. His voice is measured, precise, utterly without emotion.

"The charge is piracy. The evidence is your presence aboard this vessel, your possession of stolen Crown correspondence, and the testimony of my officers. The sentence, under Admiralty law, is death."

He produces a document — already prepared, already signed. Your name, the date, the charges, the disposition. The paperwork was done before you boarded.

"You will be transferred to Port Royal for trial," he says. "The trial will be brief. The outcome is not in question."

He turns and walks away. His footsteps recede down the corridor. The cell door does not rattle — it is too well-made for that.

You sit on the narrow bench and look at the porthole. Nassau is visible through the thick glass — the harbour, the fort, the town. All of it close enough to see, none of it close enough to reach.

Nassau will not come for you. Nassau does not know where you are, and by the time it finds out, the Crown's Justice will be at sea. Your name will appear in Admiralty records as a pirate taken in lawful action. The file will be closed. The sea will go on.

The light through the porthole dims as the sun sets. Nassau's harbour lanterns begin to glow. They are very beautiful, seen from the inside of a cell on a warship that will carry you to your execution.

They are the last beautiful thing you see.

a_36a.text:
The big man does not need a second invitation. He comes at you fast — not a brawl, a deliberate assault, the kind that ends with someone in the harbour.

Nassau is watching. It always is.

s_28.text:
You are almost ready to go when Pell calls down from the masthead.

"Captain. Sail in the channel. Small — two-masted. Not Crown and not Morrow."

A third party. Someone else has been tracking the San Cristobal. As you watch, the sloop's bow swings toward the lagoon — and toward the Adamant, sitting low in the water with everything you just recovered.

choices.text: Stand to — run out the guns
Hail them before they close
Cut cable and run for the channel now

s_28a.text: They come in anyway — three men over the rail before you can reload. Opportunists. They are not organised enough to be a real threat, but they are desperate enough to be dangerous.

s_28b.text:
"Stand off!" you call across the water. "This wreck is worked out. Nothing left worth diving for."

A pause. The sloop slows. A man at the bow cups his hands. "We'll see for ourselves."

He does not believe you. But he is not sure you are lying either. The sloop drifts, undecided.

test_luck.lucky_text: The bluff holds. The sloop comes about and threads back out through the reef channel. You watch her go, then give the order to weigh anchor before she changes her mind.
test_luck.unlucky_text: They have made up their minds. The sloop closes fast and men come over the rail.

s_28c.text:
The last man drops back into the water and swims for his sloop. You watch from the rail as they haul him aboard and make for the channel at speed — too fast, and they scrape coral on the way out. Their problem now.

Pell appears at your shoulder. "We should go."

choices.text: Agreed — weigh anchor

s_48a.text:
You are two leagues north of the Shallows when the second vessel appears — not the Fervent, a smaller brig running hard from the east, cutting across your course. She was waiting.

Ashford had two ships on this patrol. The Fervent was the signal. This one is the answer if you ignore it.

She comes alongside fast, marines at the rail.

choices.text: Crowd on sail — try to outrun her
Hold course — let them come alongside

s_48b.text: The Adamant is fast, but she is heavy today — riding low with everything in her hold. The brig gains steadily.

test_luck.lucky_text: A squall line appears on the eastern horizon and you drive into it — rain hammering the deck, visibility dropping to nothing. When the weather clears, the brig is gone. You sail north with wet rigging and a crew that does not ask questions.
test_luck.unlucky_text: She runs you down. A shot across the bow — a warning, not a miss. Her captain wants to board, not fight.

s_48c.text:
The brig comes alongside and three marines drop to the Adamant's deck — efficient, professional, weapons drawn but not threatening. Their sergeant looks at the Adamant's waterline, then at you.

"You ignored the Fervent's signal, Captain. The Commodore would like to know why."

s_48d.text:
The marines go back over the rail. The brig falls away behind you, her captain watching from the quarterdeck — noting everything, noting your colours, noting the Adamant's waterline.

Ashford will hear about this before you reach Nassau.

choices.text: Push on for Nassau — no time to waste

r_5a.text:
She is on the platform before you can reach it. Morrow and four of her people, blocking the steps. The assembled captains go quiet.

"You have no standing here," Morrow says. Her voice carries. She means it to. "You have been in Nassau three weeks. You do not get to call a council."

The captains watch. This is what they came to see — not your speech, but whether you can handle this.

choices.text: Face her down — "Nassau decides who has standing, not you"
Let her say her piece, then take the platform anyway
"I know about Thomas." — use the secret

r_5b.text:
A long moment. The captains are very still.

You step forward — not toward her, just forward, onto the same level. You do not drop your hand to your cutlass. You do not need to.

"Nassau decides," you say again. Quieter this time. Just for her.

Morrow reads you. She has spent twenty years reading captains. Whatever she finds, she steps back. Not far — a foot, maybe two. Enough.

test_luck.lucky_text: The captains shift. An old captain with a white beard begins to clap — slow, deliberate. Two others join him. Morrow turns and walks off the platform. She will not help you today. But she will not stop you either.
test_luck.unlucky_text:
Morrow does not move. "Words," she says, to the assembled captains. "A stranger with words and a fast ship, asking you to die for Nassau." She pauses. "How many of your crews does he know by name?"

It lands. You will have to work harder for this.

r_5c.text:
You wait. Let her speak. The captains listen to Morrow — you know that, you have always known that — and interrupting her here would cost more than letting her finish.

She speaks for two minutes. Measured, political, correct about every fact. You are new here. You do not have standing. Nassau has survived this long without outside help.

When she is done, you step forward.

"She is right about everything she said," you tell the assembled captains. "And Nassau is still going to fall unless someone acts. I am acting. You can watch or you can join me."

Morrow leaves without a word. The captains stay.

choices.text: Address the captains

r_5d.text:
You say it quietly, so only she can hear.

"I know about Thomas. I knew at the Shallows. I said nothing then and I am saying nothing now." A beat. "That has not changed."

Morrow goes very still. The captains cannot hear this. They see their formidable captain frozen on the platform steps and do not understand why.

She steps aside. She does not look at you as you pass. She does not leave — she stays at the back of the platform, arms folded, saying nothing for the rest of the council.

It is not support. It is the absence of opposition. Today, that is enough.

choices.text: Take the platform

r_36a.text: The Adamant comes alongside the Crown's Justice to press the attack — and Crown marines drop from the flagship's rail onto the Adamant's deck. Ashford's men do not need orders for this. Boarding is what they train for.

a_3b.text:
The Broken Wheel is the same as it always is. Smoke, rum, the door that never sits right in its frame.

But it is different now. You know the woman at the bar — Harlow, who reports to Morrow and watches the room with a professional eye. You know the man in the pressed coat — Crane, writing his figures, pretending not to notice things he absolutely notices. You know the old sailor by the hearth — Drest, who carries the weight of the San Cristóbal in his cup.

You order something and find a seat. The Adamant is waiting. The Shallows of Perdition are eight leagues south. And you have just agreed to dive a ten-year-old wreck with a woman who has kept its location secret for three years.

You drink slowly and think about what you have walked into.

choices.text: Talk to Harlow — see what she makes of all this
Talk to Crane — the Council should know the plan
Drink up and return to the Adamant

a_10b.text:
Harlow is back at the bar. She sees you coming and puts her cup down.

"Morrow doesn't usually move that fast," she says. "You made an impression."

She doesn't say it like a compliment. She says it like a piece of intelligence she is filing away.

"The Shallows of Perdition," she continues, quietly enough that the afternoon crowd can't hear. "Ten years that wreck has been sitting there. You understand why Morrow waited this long?"

She is not asking whether you understand the logistics. She is asking whether you understand Morrow.

choices.text: "She was protecting herself. The Correspondence kept Ashford off her back."
"I'm not sure I do. Tell me."
Change the subject — ask what to expect at the Shallows

a_10c.text:
Harlow studies you for a long moment.

"Most captains take longer than one conversation to figure that out." She picks up her cup. "She's going to watch you closely. So am I. You're good for Nassau or you're good for yourself — we'll find out which at the Shallows."

She turns back to the bar. The conversation is over.

choices.text: Head back to the Adamant

a_10d.text:
"Ashford came to Nassau three years ago with one ship and a polite letter," Harlow says. "Morrow met with him. After that he left — and came back this year with three."

She sets her cup down. "Whatever she told him in that meeting bought Nassau three years of breathing room. Whatever he found out — or didn't find out — ended that arrangement."

She looks at you steadily. "You're diving into the middle of something that started before you arrived. Keep that in mind."

choices.text: Head back to the Adamant

a_10e.text:
Harlow's expression shifts — the closest she gets to showing that a question landed well.

"Reef channels. The Shallows will take a ship apart if you don't know what you're doing. The San Cristóbal is in forty feet — you'll need free divers, not just your crew going over the side." She pauses. "If you don't have anyone who knows that reef, find someone before you sail. There are pilots in Nassau who've worked the southern cays."

Useful. Practical. That's Harlow.

choices.text: Thank her and head back to the Adamant
